meta.title: HHI Governance Glossary
meta.description: Canonical terminology registry defining governance concepts used in Behavioral AI Governance and Execution-Time Governance systems.

terms.definition: The infrastructure that determines how behavior accumulates, compounds, and constrains action over time.
Durable formal and informal structures shaping how authority, decisions, and accountability operate over time.
The explicit point at which authority transfers between actors during execution.
The formally assigned right to make, override, pause, or stop decisions during execution.
A role-bound capability to pause or halt system operation when governance thresholds are exceeded.
Gradual divergence between expected system behavior and observed behavior over time.
The compounding effect of repeated actions reshaping system behavior over time.
Incremental replacement of human judgment with system outputs as the default decision reference.
Transfer of decision authority to higher roles when uncertainty or risk thresholds are reached.
Progressive erosion of an organization's ability to initiate or complete escalation pathways.
Feedback process where repeated system confirmation increases user confidence regardless of objective accuracy.
Temporary fixes gradually becoming accepted as standard operational behavior.
Risk emerging through repeated behavior over time rather than discrete events.
Binding outcomes to named roles with authority prior to execution.
Loss of clear responsibility caused by distributed or mediated decision processes.
Decline in willingness or ability to override system outputs.
Repeated engagement between humans and AI systems affecting judgment, authority, and responsibility.
Risk accumulated when AI deployment outpaces governance design.
Process through which individuals begin depending on system outputs.
Governance actions applied after outcomes are questioned or failures occur.
Ongoing monitoring of governance performance rather than periodic auditing.
Traceability of authority and responsibility across time.
Principle that humans retain final decision authority and ethical accountability.
Gradual shift in decision authority away from intended governance structures.
Misalignment between governance policy and operational behavior.
Breakdown of governance mechanisms controlling system behavior.
Point where governance intervention becomes necessary.
Transfer of interpretive responsibility from humans to automated systems.
Perception of governance control without real enforcement.
Condition where authority responsibilities exceed human operational capacity.
Metric measuring alignment between system outputs and governance terminology.
Indicator measuring stability in human-system interaction patterns.
Metric measuring long-term consistency of governance behavior.
Metric evaluating alignment between authority structures and governance design.
Monitoring interface displaying human-system interaction dynamics.
Operational signals measuring governance performance.
Recorded sequence of human-system interactions.
System composed of human, technical, and organizational components.
Governance mechanisms operating during system execution.
Structural components enforcing governance rules.
Observable interface where governance interactions occur.
